section.title: Naomi and Ruth Return
section.sectionRationale: This movement completes chapter one: Naomi hears the LORD has visited His people, sets out for home, and releases her daughters-in-law. Orpah turns back, but Ruth clings with a famous vow of covenant loyalty. They arrive in Bethlehem where Naomi pours out her bitterness. It is one unit built on the repeated word return and the contrast between turning back and cleaving.
section.thesis: As Naomi turns home empty and bitter, a Moabite widow binds herself to Naomi's people and Naomi's God in astonishing covenant loyalty, so that the barley harvest and Ruth's presence become the first quiet signs that God has not abandoned the emptied.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the road home and the plea to turn back; Orpah's departure and Ruth's vow; the arrival in Bethlehem; and Naomi's lament, Call me Mara.

units.label: The road home and the plea to turn back
units.note: the LORD has visited His people

verses.text: When Naomi heard in Moab that the LORD had attended to His people by providing them with food, she and her daughters-in-law prepared to leave the land of Moab.
Accompanied by her two daughters-in-law, she left the place where she had been living and set out on the road leading back to the land of Judah.
Then Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law, “Go back, each of you to your mother’s home. May the LORD show you {{chesed|loving devotion}}, as you have shown to your dead and to me.
May the LORD enable each of you to find rest in the home of your new husband.” And she kissed them as they wept aloud
and said, “Surely we will {{shuv|return}} with you to your people.”

units.label: Orpah turns back, Ruth clings
units.note: your people will be my people

verses.text: But Naomi replied, “Return home, my daughters. Why would you go with me? Are there still sons in my womb to become your husbands?
Return home, my daughters. Go on, for I am too old to have another husband. Even if I thought there was hope for me to have a husband tonight and to bear sons,
would you wait for them to grow up? Would you refrain from having husbands? No, my daughters, it is much more bitter for me than for you, because the hand of the LORD has gone out against me.”
Again they wept aloud, and Orpah kissed her mother-in-law goodbye, but Ruth {{dabaq|clung}} to her.
“Look,” said Naomi, “your sister-in-law has gone back to her people and her gods; follow her back home.”

But Ruth replied: “Do not urge me to leave you or to turn from following you. For wherever you go, I will go, and wherever you live, I will live; your people will be my people, and your God will be my God.
Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. May the LORD punish me, and ever so severely, if anything but death separates you and me.”
When Naomi saw that Ruth was determined to go with her, she stopped trying to persuade her.

units.label: Arrival in Bethlehem and Naomi's lament

verses.text: So Naomi and Ruth traveled until they came to Bethlehem. When they entered Bethlehem, the whole town was stirred because of them, and the women of the town exclaimed, “Can this be Naomi?”
“Do not call me Naomi,” she replied. “Call me {{mara|Mara}}, because the {{shaddai|Almighty}} has dealt quite bitterly with me.
I went away full, but the LORD has brought me back empty. Why call me Naomi? After all, the LORD has testified against me, and the Almighty has afflicted me.”
So Naomi returned from the land of Moab with her daughter-in-law Ruth the Moabitess. And they arrived in Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love, loyal kindness that goes beyond mere duty. It is the great theme of Ruth, shown by Ruth to Naomi, by Boaz to both women, and supremely by the LORD to them all. The BSB footnote at verse 8 notes its wide range: love, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant.

shuv.gloss: To return, turn back, or turn around. The verb sounds through chapter one again and again as Naomi returns to Bethlehem and urges her daughters-in-law to turn back to Moab. Ruth's refusal to turn back is the hinge of the chapter; the same root elsewhere in Scripture carries the sense of turning to God in repentance and faith.

dabaq.gloss: To cling, cleave, hold fast, stick to. It is the very word used in Genesis 2:24 of a husband cleaving to his wife. Here Ruth cleaves to Naomi with a loyalty as strong as a marriage bond: Orpah kisses and leaves, but Ruth clings and will not let go.

mara.gloss: Bitter. Naomi renames herself Mara, bitter, in place of Naomi, pleasant, confessing that the Almighty has dealt bitterly with her. Her lament is honest and raw, yet the reader already sees the barley harvest and faithful Ruth as signs that her emptiness is not the end of the story.

shaddai.gloss: The Almighty, an ancient title for God stressing His overwhelming power and all-sufficiency. Naomi twice names God as Shaddai, the One she believes has afflicted her. The same title fills the book of Job, another believer wrestling honestly with God's hidden hand in suffering; and the Almighty who empties Naomi is the very One quietly working her rescue.

basic.context: Naomi hears that the famine in Judah has lifted and decides to go home. On the road she urges both widowed daughters-in-law to turn back to Moab and find new husbands. Orpah tearfully goes back, but Ruth refuses, binding herself to Naomi with a beautiful vow: your people will be my people, your God my God. They reach Bethlehem, where Naomi tells the women to call her Mara, bitter, because she left full and has come back empty.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative that turns on a single repeated word, return or turn back, and on the contrast between Orpah who turns back and Ruth who clings. Ruth's vow is one of the great confessions of loyalty in the Bible. Naomi's bitter complaint is honest lament, not unbelief to be scolded; she rightly sees God's hand, even if she cannot yet see His kindness.
basic.originalLanguages: The word shuv, to return or turn back, drums through the chapter as Naomi returns and presses the others to go back. Ruth instead dabaq, clings, the same word for a husband cleaving to his wife. Naomi asks to be called Mara, bitter, and names God as Shaddai, the Almighty, who she feels has emptied her. And she prays chesed, loving devotion, over the very daughters she is sending away.
basic.typology: Ruth the Moabite outsider, joining herself by faith to the LORD and His people, is a living picture of the nations being grafted in, fulfilled when the gospel goes to the Gentiles. Her covenant vow foreshadows the deeper cleaving of the redeemed to God, and the barley harvest hints at the provision God is about to pour out.
basic.christ: Ruth, an outsider brought near, stands in the line that leads to Christ, and her welcome into God's people previews the day when people from every nation would be included through Jesus. Naomi's move from empty to full, still only hinted here, traces the gospel shape of loss reversed by a redeemer, which Christ accomplishes in full.
basic.god: God is at work behind the scenes: He has visited His people with bread, He is drawing Ruth to Himself, and He is steering Naomi home just as the harvest begins. Naomi feels only His heavy hand, yet the reader sees His faithful loving devotion quietly surrounding her even in her bitterness.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 23 marked Moabites as outsiders, which makes Ruth's inclusion striking. Matthew 1 names Ruth in Jesus' family tree. And Ephesians 2 describes how those once far off are brought near, the very thing happening to Ruth.
basic.oneStory: The one great story of God gathering a people to Himself moves forward as a Moabite widow says, your God will be my God. Naomi comes home empty and bitter, but Ruth's loyalty and the opening harvest are the first signs that God is about to fill the empty, foreshadowing the Redeemer who welcomes outsiders and turns mourning into joy.

context.literary: The completion of the chapter one arc, structured around the leitmotif of returning and turning back, repeated more than ten times. The scene pivots on the parallel choices of Orpah and Ruth, with Ruth's poetic vow at its center. It closes with Naomi's lament and the pointed note that they arrive at the beginning of the barley harvest, a hook into chapter two.
context.historical: A widow without husband or sons was among the most vulnerable people in the ancient world, dependent on the kindness of others and the provisions of the law. Moab lay east of the Dead Sea and was long an enemy of Israel with its own gods, notably Chemosh; for Ruth to renounce her people and gods for the LORD was a costly, decisive break. The barley harvest fell in spring, around April.
context.canonical: Ruth's confession joins the great biblical theme of outsiders drawn into covenant with the LORD, from Rahab to the Gentile mission. Her arrival in Bethlehem at harvest sets the stage for the redemption that carries the line toward David and Christ, tying this chapter into the whole sweep of salvation history.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To showcase Ruth's covenant loyalty as the turning point of the chapter, to voice Naomi's honest lament under God's hand, and to signal, through the harvest, that God's provision is already beginning even as Naomi feels only emptiness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Naomi's bitterness and Ruth's vow. It does not command us to imitate Orpah's return, nor does it condemn her; rather it holds up Ruth's clinging loyalty and, in Naomi, models honest lament that still names God as sovereign.

debates.question: Is Naomi's lament in verses 20 and 21 a failure of faith or faithful honesty before God?

views.label: Faithful lament
views.case: Naomi speaks truly: God is sovereign over her suffering, and she brings her bitterness straight to Him rather than denying it, in the pattern of the Psalms and Job. This is faith wrestling, not faith abandoned.

views.label: Faith clouded by grief
views.case: She calls herself empty while Ruth, who will prove better than seven sons, stands beside her, and she cannot yet see the kindness of God at work; her words show real but limited sight, a faith dimmed by pain.

debates.bearing: Both readings honor the text: God welcomes honest lament, and He is also kindly at work beyond what the sufferer can presently see, so grief and hope can be held together.

debates.question: Why does the narrator highlight that Ruth is a Moabitess?

views.label: To magnify grace to the outsider
views.case: Moab was an enemy nation under a curse of exclusion, so naming Ruth a Moabitess again and again underscores the wonder that she is brought into God's people and into the Messiah's line by faith.

views.label: To set up the theme of inclusion
views.case: The repeated label prepares the reader for the surprise of chapter four, where a Moabite widow becomes the great-grandmother of King David, showing God's purpose to gather the nations.

debates.bearing: Either way, Ruth's Moabite identity is not incidental; it displays the reach of God's covenant mercy beyond Israel, a foretaste of the gospel to all peoples.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is built on the verb shuv, to return or turn back, which recurs relentlessly as Naomi returns to Judah and repeatedly urges Orpah and Ruth to go back to Moab. Against that tide of turning back stands Ruth, who dabaq, clings, to Naomi, the same verb used of a husband cleaving to his wife in Genesis 2:24, marking her loyalty as a covenant bond. Naomi prays chesed, loving devotion, over her daughters even as she releases them, naming the very quality that will define the whole book. Arriving home, she asks to be called Mara, bitter, not Naomi, pleasant, and twice names God as Shaddai, the Almighty, confessing His sovereign hand in her emptiness while the barley harvest quietly announces His coming provision.

typology.type: Ruth the Moabitess joined by faith to the LORD and His people
typology.fulfillment: The nations grafted into the people of God through faith, fulfilled as the gospel goes to the Gentiles, Ephesians 2:11-13.

typology.type: Ruth's covenant vow, your God will be my God
typology.fulfillment: The believer's cleaving to God in Christ, and the new covenant promise, I will be their God and they will be my people.

typology.type: Naomi returning empty at the start of harvest
typology.fulfillment: The reversal of emptiness by a redeemer, a pattern completed when Christ turns the mourning of His people into fullness of joy.

study.christ: Ruth's confession makes her a charter member of a people gathered from the nations, the very thing Christ accomplishes when He brings those who were far off near by His blood, Ephesians 2:13. As a Moabite outsider welcomed into Israel and placed in the Messiah's genealogy, Matthew 1:5, Ruth previews the inclusion of the Gentiles in the gospel. And Naomi's journey from full to empty, soon to be filled again through a kinsman-redeemer, traces in miniature the movement from loss to redemption that Jesus works for all who trust Him.
study.god: The one action explicitly credited to God in this movement is mercy: the LORD has attended to His people by giving them bread, and He is drawing a Moabite widow to Himself and guiding Naomi home just as the harvest opens. Naomi feels only the heavy hand of the Almighty, and she is not wrong that He is sovereign over her sorrow; but the reader sees what she cannot, that His loving devotion, chesed, already surrounds her. This is the God who works quietly and faithfully beneath our darkest seasons, and who delights to fill the empty.

crossReferences.note: Moabites were excluded from the assembly, which makes Ruth's inclusion by faith all the more striking.
Ruth, who loves Naomi, is later called better to her than seven sons, answering Naomi's cry of emptiness.
Those once far off, foreigners to the covenant, are brought near by the blood of Christ, as Ruth is brought near here.
Ruth is named in the genealogy of Jesus, a Moabitess in the royal Messianic line.

study.oneStory: The Bible's story of a God who gathers a people to Himself takes a decisive step as a Moabite widow renounces her gods and clings to the LORD: your people will be my people, your God will be my God. Naomi comes home empty and bitter, but Ruth's loyalty and the opening barley harvest are the first quiet promises that God will fill the empty again. It all leans forward to the Redeemer who welcomes outsiders, turns mourning into joy, and secures the line that leads to David and to Jesus.

sermon.title: God of the Ordinary
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Ruth, this message treats Ruth 1:19-22, the return to Bethlehem and Naomi's lament, Call me Mara, showing God's providence at work in ordinary loss and homecoming. A faithful, expository handling of the close of this movement.

quiz.q: What did Ruth vow to Naomi on the road to Judah?

options.text: Where you go I will go; your people will be my people, and your God will be my God.
options.note: Ruth 1:16-17 records her famous vow of covenant loyalty and faith.

options.text: That she would find Naomi a new husband in Bethlehem.
options.note: Ruth pledged herself to Naomi, not to arranging a marriage for her.

options.text: That she would return to Moab if life in Judah proved hard.
options.note: She vowed the opposite: only death would separate them.

options.text: That she would keep worshiping the gods of Moab.
options.note: She renounced Moab's gods and embraced the LORD as her God.

quiz.q: How did Orpah and Ruth respond differently to Naomi's plea?

options.text: Orpah turned back to Moab, but Ruth clung to Naomi and would not leave.
options.note: Ruth 1:14 contrasts Orpah's kiss goodbye with Ruth clinging to Naomi.

options.text: Both turned back, but Ruth returned later.
options.note: Ruth never turned back; she clung from the start, unlike Orpah.

options.text: Both refused and went with Naomi.
options.note: Only Ruth went on; Orpah returned to her people.

options.text: Orpah went with Naomi and Ruth stayed in Moab.
options.note: It was the reverse: Ruth went and Orpah stayed.

quiz.q: Why did Naomi ask to be called Mara?

options.text: Mara means bitter, and she felt the Almighty had dealt bitterly with her, emptying her of husband and sons.
options.note: Ruth 1:20-21 records her lament that she went out full and came back empty.

options.text: Mara was the name of her homeland in Moab.
options.note: Mara is not a place name; it means bitter and expresses her grief.

options.text: She wanted to honor a relative named Mara.
options.note: The name is chosen for its meaning, bitter, not for a relative.

options.text: She had converted to a new religion.
options.note: The name change reflects grief, not a change of faith.

quiz.q: What detail at the end of chapter one hints at hope to come?

options.text: They arrive in Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.
options.note: Ruth 1:22 signals God's provision beginning, setting up the gleaning of chapter two.

options.text: Naomi is immediately given a new husband.
options.note: No new husband is given; the hopeful sign is the harvest and Ruth's presence.

options.text: The famine returns to Moab.
options.note: The hopeful note is provision in Judah, not disaster in Moab.

options.text: Orpah changes her mind and follows them.
options.note: Orpah does not return; the hope lies in the harvest and in Ruth.

quiz.q: What is striking about Ruth being repeatedly called a Moabitess?

options.text: Moab was an enemy nation, so her welcome into God's people shows the wide reach of His grace.
options.note: Her inclusion previews the gathering of the nations, fulfilled in the gospel.

options.text: It shows she never truly belonged and was only tolerated.
options.note: She is fully welcomed and even placed in the Messiah's line, not merely tolerated.

options.text: It proves the book is only about Moab, not Israel.
options.note: The book is set in Israel and points to David; her Moabite origin magnifies grace.

options.text: It is an unimportant detail with no meaning.
options.note: The repeated label is deliberate, highlighting grace to an outsider.

questions: Ruth gave up her homeland, her people, and her old gods to follow the LORD. What has following God cost you, and what has it been worth?
Orpah made a reasonable, safe choice to go back; Ruth made a costly choice to cling. Where is God calling you to costly loyalty rather than the safe road back?
Naomi brought her bitterness honestly to God rather than pretending. How does her example free you to lament truthfully while still trusting Him?
Ruth, an outsider, was welcomed into God's people by faith. How does this shape the way you welcome those who feel far off from God?
God had quietly provided bread and was drawing Ruth in, even while Naomi felt empty. Where might God be at work in your life in ways you have not yet noticed?

translationNotes.note: Loving devotion renders the Hebrew chesed, covenant loyalty and steadfast love; the BSB footnote notes its range of love, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and covenant loyalty.
Clung renders dabaq, the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 of a husband cleaving to his wife, marking Ruth's loyalty as covenant-strong.
Naomi means pleasant and Mara means bitter; the wordplay turns her own name into a lament. Almighty renders Shaddai, also in verse 21.
I went away full but the LORD has brought me back empty frames the book's central reversal, from emptiness to the fullness God will provide.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ruth 1:6-22. Ruth's poetic vow in verses 16-17, set as poetry in the source, is rendered here as running prose within each verse per house style.
